title: Octets, Byte Streams, Fragmentation, Buffering, and Partial I/O · Program Defense
summary: A security review compares near-miss parser decisions for octets, byte streams, fragmentation, buffering, and partial i/o and requires the smallest strict fragment-safe byte-stream reader and writer contract with changed-input evidence. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for fragment-safe byte-stream reader and writer contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Keep protocol data as octets until grammar permits text interpretation and distinguish ASCII tokens, opaque content, and invalid control bytes.
Interpret Read results using both byte count and error, including data with io.EOF, zero-byte reads, and temporary fragmentation.
Complete bounded output across short writes while retaining progress, error, and ownership evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Fragmentation Invariance
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A quiz protocol team handles case 6671 in a signed webhook receiver behind an HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 gateway. Build deterministic Go evidence to preserve partial bytes while distinguishing fragmentation from incomplete input; preserve this operating constraint: the browser model cannot open a socket or claim packet-level proof; then lower one byte or time budget and prove rejection occurs before allocation or dispatch. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Prove the same bounded message result for every legal segmentation of an identical byte sequence.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?

Evidence target: Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Fragmentation Invariance.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for prove the same bounded message result for every legal segmentation of an identical byte sequence. For Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Fragmentation Invariance, keep Fragmentation Invariance connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

steps.title: Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Fragmentation Invariance
steps.instruction:
Write the quiz evidence note for Fragmentation Invariance: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A quiz protocol team handles case 6678 in a regional status service with slow assistive and low-bandwidth clients. Build deterministic Go evidence to preserve partial bytes while distinguishing fragmentation from incomplete input; preserve this operating constraint: the browser model cannot open a socket or claim packet-level proof; then append a second pipelined message and prove the first parser leaves the exact residual bytes. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Prove the same bounded message result for every legal segmentation of an identical byte sequence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Prove the same bounded message result for every legal segmentation of an identical byte sequence.
